senses.gloss: to lie, to not tell the truth; to lie to (someone)

examples.english: He lied to me in his speech.

examples.text:
وَكَمِ ٱنْتَشْتَ بِٱلسُّيُوفِ مِنَ الدَّهْـ / ـرِ أَسِيرًا وَبِٱلنَّوَالِ مُقِلَّا
عَدَّهَا نُصْرَةً عَلَيْهِ فَلَمَّا / صَالَ خَتْلًا رَآهُ أَدْرَكَ تَبْلَا
كَذَبَتْهُ ظُنُونُهُ أَنْتَ تُبْلِيـ / ـهِ وَتَبْقَى فِي نِعْمَةٍ لَيْسَ تَبَلَّى

senses.gloss: to lie, to not tell the truth; to lie about (عَلَى (ʕalā))

examples.text: فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن كَذَبَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ وَكَذَّبَ بِٱلصِّدْقِ إِذْ جَاءَهُ

senses.gloss: to allege of lying, to disbelieve someone

examples.text: وَأَخِي هَارُونُ هُوَ أَفْصَحُ مِنِّي لِسَانًا فَأَرْسِلْهُ مَعِيَ رِدْءًا يُصَدِّقُنِيۖ إِنِّي أَخَافُ أَن يُكَذِّبُونِ
examples.english: My brother Aaron is more eloquent than me in speech. Therefor send him with me as a helper to confirm me. Lo! I fear that they will give the lie to me.

senses.gloss: to accuse or tell that something is false or deceitful, to disbelieve

examples.english: They called Our revelations false with strong denial.

examples.text:
إِنَّ سَعْيَكُمْ لَشَتَّى
 فَأَمَّا مَنْ أَعْطَى وَٱتَّقَى
 وَصَدَّقَ بِٱلْحُسْنَى
 فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْيُسْرَى
 وَأَمَّا مَن بَخِلَ وَٱسْتَغْنَى
 وَكَذَّبَ بِٱلْحُسْنَى
 فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْعُسْرَى
examples.english:
Lo! your effort is dispersed (toward divers ends).
As for him who giveth and is dutiful (toward Allah)
And believeth in goodness;
Surely We will ease his way unto the state of ease.
But as for him who hoardeth and deemeth himself independent,
And disbelieveth in goodness;
Surely We will ease his way unto adversity.

senses.gloss: to be inactive, to be deactivated

examples.english: And they brought his shirt and stained it with false blood.

etymology.text: Cognate with Hebrew כָּזָב (kazáv, “to lie, to deceive, to mislead”) and Classical Syriac ܟܕܒܐ (kaddɑvɑ, “liar”).

The saying what resembles a lie, not meaning anything but the truth.
Such is meant in the trad.,
Abraham said three sayings resembling lies;
he being veracious in the three. — Third,
The saying what is untrue by mistake,

being covered by the stallion, raised her tail, and then returned without conceiving.

he imagined the thing contrary to its real state.

He found his hopes to be false,
See how they lied against themselves,
see how their hope hath proved false,
thought that they had been disappointed of the fulfilment of the promise made to them.
So accord. to one reading. Accord. to another reading, the verb is ↓
: [in which case, the meaning of the words appears to be, “ They knew that they had been pronounced liars ” by the people to whom they were sent]. (TA.) There are also two other readings; ↓
: accord. to the former, the verb refers to the people to whom the apostles were sent; and
means “ they knew: ” accord. to the latter, the words mean, “ They (the people above mentioned) thought that they (the apostles) had broken their promise. ” (Jel.) ―         -b15-

his soul made him to desire things, and to conceive hopes, that could scarcely come to pass.

The noun following is put in the nom. case accord. to the dial. of El-Yemen; and in the acc. accord. to the dial. of Mudar; or, as some say, is correctly put in the nom. only. (TA.) You say,
take to, such and such things.
It is an extr. phrase. (ISk.) You also say,
IAar. cites the following verse of Khidásh Ibn-Zuheyr, [in which he tauntingly compares a people to ticks]:

ka*abotu Ealayokumo A^awo EiduwniY waEal~iluwA
                      
                     
                        biYa AlA^aroDa waAlA^aqowaAma qirodaAna mawoZabaA
Keep ye to me: threaten me, and soothe by
me the land and the peoples, O ticks of Mowdhab!
]: meaning Keep ye to me, and to satirizing me, when ye are on a journey, and traverse the land mentioning me. (TA.) In like manner,

body.text: , in a trad. respecting the proper days for being cupped, signifies
Keep thou to Sunday and Thursday,
(IAth, Z.) The verb is thus used after the manner of a proverb, and is invariable [as to tense], being constantly in the pret. tense, connected [literally or virtually, when explained by
,] only with the person addressed, and in the sense of the imperative.
brisk, and animate thee, instigate thee,
(Z.). [A trad. of 'Omar, quoted below, presents another instance to which this signification is said to apply.] ―         -b19-  Or
denotes instigation, or incitement, of the person addressed, to keep to the thing that is mentioned; as in the saying of the Arabs,
but the explanation of this is, (
i. e., in his representation of its evil qualites &c.; which is equivalent to saying,
.] In like manner, the saying of 'Omar,
Keep ye to the performance of the pilgrimage, &c.:
the pilgrimage hath erred to thee

in his representation of it: therefore it means as above]. (IAmb.) Accord. to IAmb the noun signifying the object of instigation [which may also be called the cause thereof] cannot be rightly put in the acc. case: if so put, the verb is without an agent. (TA.) [But see what is said on this point in the remarks on the trad. of 'Omar below.] ―         -b20-  Or the verb in a case of this kind signifies
